Tomato_Early_blight.name: Early Blight
Tomato_Early_blight.description: A fungal disease producing dark, concentric 'target-ring' spots on older, lower leaves first, which can spread upward.
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings, resembling a target
Yellowing of leaf tissue surrounding the spots
Symptoms typically begin on older, lower leaves first
causes: Alternaria solani fungus, thriving in warm, humid conditions
Overhead irrigation and prolonged leaf wetness
treatment: Apply a fungicide labeled for early blight (e.g. chlorothalonil, copper)
Mulch around the base to prevent soil splash onto leaves
prevention: Stake or cage plants for better air circulation
Water at the base, early in the day, to let foliage dry quickly

Tomato_Healthy.name: Healthy
Tomato_Healthy.description: This leaf shows no visible signs of disease or pest damage — the plant appears to be in good health.
symptoms: Uniform green coloring with no spots, curling, or wilting
No visible mold, webbing, or lesions
treatment: No treatment needed — continue standard care and monitoring
prevention: Keep up consistent watering, good airflow, and regular inspection
Rotate crops each season as general good practice
Monitor periodically so early symptoms are caught quickly

Tomato_leaf_late_blight.name: Late Blight
Tomato_leaf_late_blight.description: An aggressive, fast-spreading disease causing large, irregular greasy-looking patches that turn brown and can destroy a crop within days.
symptoms: Large, irregular, water-soaked patches that quickly turn brown
White fuzzy mold on the underside of leaves in humid conditions
Rapid wilting and collapse of whole leaves or stems
causes: Phytophthora infestans (the same pathogen behind the Irish potato famine)
Wind-blown spores from infected plants nearby
treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately — do not compost them
Apply a fungicide preventatively at the first regional report of late blight
Improve drainage and airflow around remaining plants
prevention: Plant resistant tomato varieties where available
Avoid overhead watering, especially in the evening
Monitor local blight forecasts during humid seasons

Tomato_leaf_yellow_curl_virus.name: Yellow Leaf Curl Virus
Tomato_leaf_yellow_curl_virus.description: A viral disease causing upward curling, yellowing, and stunted growth of leaves, severely reducing fruit yield.
symptoms: Upward curling and yellowing of leaf edges
Stunted, bushy plant growth with smaller leaves
Significant reduction in flowering and fruit set
causes: Transmitted exclusively by the silverleaf whitefly
Nearby infected plants acting as a virus reservoir
Warm climates where whiteflies thrive year-round
treatment: There is no cure once a plant is infected — remove and destroy infected plants
Control whitefly populations with insecticidal soap or approved insecticides
Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and reduce whitefly numbers
prevention: Use reflective mulches or fine mesh row covers to deter whiteflies
Remove weeds nearby that can host whiteflies

Tomato_mold_leaf.name: Leaf Mold
Tomato_mold_leaf.description: A fungal disease showing pale green or yellow spots on the upper leaf surface with olive-green to grayish mold on the underside.
symptoms: Pale green or yellow spots on the upper leaf surface
Olive-green to grayish fuzzy mold on the underside of leaves
Leaves may curl, wither, and drop in advanced cases
causes: Passalora fulva fungus, favored by high humidity (above 85%)
Poor ventilation, common in greenhouses and dense plantings
treatment: Increase ventilation and reduce humidity around plants
Apply a fungicide if the infection is spreading
prevention: Space plants generously and prune for airflow
Water at the soil line, not on foliage
Choose resistant varieties for greenhouse growing

Tomato_septora_leaf_spot.name: Septoria Leaf Spot
Tomato_septora_leaf_spot.description: A fungal disease producing many small, circular spots with dark borders and light gray centers, usually starting on lower leaves.
symptoms: Numerous small, circular spots with dark margins and gray centers
Tiny black specks (fungal fruiting bodies) visible within spots
Heavily spotted leaves turn yellow, then brown, and drop early
causes: Septoria lycopersici fungus, spread by rain splash and wet tools
Leftover infected plant debris in soil
treatment: Remove infected leaves as soon as spots appear
Apply a fungicide labeled for Septoria leaf spot
Clear fallen leaves and debris around the plant
prevention: Rotate crops for at least one year
Mulch to prevent soil splashing onto lower leaves
